name: Work Opportunity Tax Credit (WOTC)
filing_deadline: with annual tax return; Form 8850 must be submitted to state workforce agency within 28 days of hire date
eligibility_text: The Work Opportunity Tax Credit (WOTC) is available to employers who hire individuals from targeted groups that have consistently faced significant barriers to employment. Targeted groups include: qualified veterans (especially those receiving SNAP benefits, with service-connected disabilities, or unemployed for at least 4 weeks), individuals receiving Temporary Assistance for Needy Families (TANF), Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP/food stamp) recipients aged 18-39, designated community residents living in Empowerment Zones or Rural Renewal Counties, vocational rehabilitation referrals, ex-felons hired within one year of release or conviction, summer youth employees aged 16-17 who work between May 1 and September 15, Supplemental Security Income (SSI) recipients, and qualified long-term unemployment recipients. The credit ranges from $2,400 per qualified employee for most groups, up to $9,600 for certain qualified veterans. Employers must obtain certification from the state workforce agency by submitting Form 8850 within 28 calendar days of the new hire's start date.
documentation_required: ETA Form 9061 or 9062 for individual certification

name: Research and Development (R&D) Tax Credit
filing_deadline: with annual return; under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, retroactive elections for tax years 2022-2024 must be filed by July 4, 2026
eligibility_text: The Research & Development Tax Credit (also called the Research Credit or Section 41 Credit) rewards businesses for investing in qualified research activities conducted in the United States. Qualified research expenditures include: wages paid to employees performing or directly supervising qualified research, supplies used in qualified research, contracted research expenses (65% qualifies), and rental costs of computers used for qualified research. Qualified research must satisfy a four-part test: (1) the activity must aim to develop or improve a product, process, software, technique, formula, or invention; (2) it must be technological in nature, relying on principles of physical, biological, engineering, or computer science; (3) it must be intended to eliminate uncertainty about capability, methodology, or design; and (4) it must involve a process of experimentation, including evaluation of alternatives. Common qualifying activities include software development, manufacturing process improvements, formulation work, and prototype development. Eligible small businesses (under $5M in gross receipts and no gross receipts more than 5 years ago) may apply up to $500,000 of the credit against their payroll tax liability instead of income tax. Under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, qualifying small businesses can retroactively claim R&D credits for tax years 2022, 2023, and 2024 — but only if elections are filed by July 4, 2026.

name: Credit for Small Employer Health Insurance Premiums
eligibility_text: Small employers may claim a credit of up to 50% of the cost of health insurance premiums paid for their employees (35% for tax-exempt employers). To qualify, the employer must: (1) have fewer than 25 full-time equivalent employees; (2) pay average annual wages of less than approximately $58,000 per FTE (indexed annually); (3) pay at least 50% of the premium cost for each enrolled employee; and (4) purchase coverage through the Small Business Health Options Program (SHOP) Marketplace. The full credit is available to employers with 10 or fewer FTEs and average wages of $27,000 or less; the credit phases out as either number rises. The credit is available for any two consecutive years for which the employer offers SHOP coverage.

name: Employer Credit for Paid Family and Medical Leave (Section 45S)
eligibility_text: Employers who provide paid family and medical leave to qualifying employees may claim a tax credit ranging from 12.5% to 25% of wages paid during leave. To qualify, employers must have a written policy providing at least two weeks of paid leave per year to all qualifying full-time employees (prorated for part-time), and pay at least 50% of the employee's normal wages during leave. Employees must have been employed for at least one year and earned no more than approximately $84,000 in the prior year (2024 indexed limit). Qualifying leave reasons match those under the federal Family and Medical Leave Act: birth or adoption of a child, care for a spouse, child, or parent with a serious health condition, the employee's own serious health condition, or qualifying military exigency. The credit percentage scales with the wage replacement rate: 12.5% if leave pays 50% of wages, increasing 0.25 points per percentage point up to 25% at 100% wage replacement. Maximum 12 weeks of paid leave per employee per year qualify.

name: Credit for Small Employer Pension Plan Startup Costs
filing_deadline: with annual return for first 3 years of plan
eligibility_text: Small employers (fewer than 100 employees who received at least $5,000 in compensation in the preceding year) that establish a new SEP, SIMPLE IRA, or qualified retirement plan may claim a credit equal to 50% of qualified startup costs (100% for employers with 50 or fewer employees, under SECURE 2.0). The credit is capped at the greater of $500 or the lesser of $5,000 or $250 per non-highly compensated employee eligible to participate. The credit is available for the first three years of the plan. Qualified startup costs include expenses to set up and administer the plan and to educate employees about the plan. Under SECURE 2.0, an additional credit equal to a percentage of employer contributions (up to $1,000 per employee) is available to employers with 50 or fewer employees during the first five years.
documentation_required: invoices for plan setup and administration

name: Disabled Access Credit
eligibility_text: Eligible small businesses may claim a credit of 50% of eligible access expenditures over $250 and up to $10,250 (resulting in a maximum credit of $5,000) for expenses incurred to comply with the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA). To qualify, the business must have either: gross receipts of $1 million or less in the preceding tax year, OR no more than 30 full-time employees. Eligible expenses include: removing physical, communication, or transportation barriers; providing qualified interpreters or readers; producing accessible formats of printed material (braille, audio, large print); providing or modifying equipment for use by individuals with disabilities; and similar accommodations. Routine maintenance, repairs, or expansions don't qualify; only expenditures specifically intended to make a facility accessible.
documentation_required: documentation that expenses comply with ADA

name: California Research and Development Credit
eligibility_text: California's Research Credit follows the federal Section 41 R&D credit framework but with state-specific rules and rates. The credit equals 15% of qualified research expenses exceeding a base amount, plus 24% of basic research payments. Qualified research must be conducted within California and meet the four-part federal test: a permitted purpose (developing/improving a product, process, software, technique, formula, or invention), technological in nature, intended to eliminate technical uncertainty, and involving a process of experimentation. Unlike the federal credit, California does not allow the credit to offset payroll tax. Unused credits may be carried forward indefinitely until exhausted. Qualifying expenses include in-house research wages, supplies consumed in research, and 65% of contract research conducted in California. Software development for sale or license generally qualifies; internal-use software faces a higher hurdle.
documentation_required: payroll records for in-state research employees

name: Texas Franchise Tax R&D Credit
eligibility_text: Texas allows a Franchise Tax Credit for research and development activities conducted in Texas. Taxable entities may claim a credit equal to 5% of the difference between qualified research expenses incurred during the period and 50% of the average amount of qualified research expenses incurred during the three preceding tax periods. If the taxable entity contracts with a Texas public or private institution of higher education for the performance of qualified research, the rate is 6.25%. Qualified research expenses follow the federal Section 41 definition. Alternatively, qualifying entities may elect a sales tax exemption on the purchase of depreciable tangible personal property used in qualified research instead of the franchise tax credit. Unused credits may be carried forward up to 20 years.
documentation_required: contracts with Texas universities if claiming higher rate

name: New York Investment Tax Credit (ITC)
eligibility_text: New York's Investment Tax Credit allows businesses to claim a credit equal to 5% (4% for individuals/partnerships) of the cost or other basis of qualified property placed in service in New York State. Qualified property includes tangible personal property and other tangible property (including buildings and structural components) that is principally used in: production of goods by manufacturing, processing, assembly, refining, mining, extracting, farming, agriculture, horticulture, floriculture, viticulture, or commercial fishing; or research and development. The property must be depreciable, have a useful life of four years or more, acquired by purchase, situated in New York State, and used principally in qualifying activity for at least 12 consecutive months. An additional employment incentive credit (1.5%-2.5%) is available if employment increases. Unused credit can be carried forward up to 10 years; new businesses may receive a refund.
documentation_required: documentation of property's NY location and qualifying use

name: Federal Opportunity Zone Investment (Qualified Opportunity Fund)
filing_deadline: with return for the year of investment
eligibility_text: The Federal Opportunity Zones program allows investors to defer and reduce capital gains taxes by investing eligible gains in a Qualified Opportunity Fund (QOF) within 180 days of realization. The QOF must hold at least 90% of its assets in Qualified Opportunity Zone Property — businesses or property located in one of 8,764 designated low-income census tracts across the U.S. and territories. Tax benefits include: (1) deferral of recognition of the original gain until December 31, 2026 or sale of the QOF interest, whichever is earlier; (2) full elimination of capital gains tax on appreciation of the QOF investment if held for at least 10 years. To qualify as Qualified Opportunity Zone Business Property, the property must be acquired after December 31, 2017, used in a trade or business of the QOF, and substantially all use must occur in the Opportunity Zone. The property must be either original-use or substantially improved (improvements equal to at least the basis of the property within 30 months).
documentation_required: evidence of Opportunity Zone property acquisition and use
